---
title: "Shapiro–Wilk on regression residuals: research notes"
author: Sabine Schilling (notes compiled with Claude)
date: "2026-05-29"
output:
  bookdown::html_document2:
    base_format: rmarkdown::html_vignette
    toc: true
    number_sections: true
    toc_depth: 3
---

# Purpose of this document

Internal reference, not part of the CRAN vignette. Records the
literature review and theoretical reasoning behind the package's
choice of residual type fed to `shapiro.test()` and `ad.test()` in
the linear-model assumption check.

Current package behaviour (as of 2026-05-29):
`shapiro.test(raw_residuals)` and `ad.test(raw_residuals)`, called at

- `R/visstat_core.R:337`
- `R/vis_lm_assumptions.R:90`
- `R/test_and_visuals.R:813, 841, 897`
- `R/vis_numeric.R:127`

# Question

When checking the normality assumption of a fitted linear model,
should the Shapiro–Wilk (SW) and Anderson–Darling (AD) tests be
applied to

- raw residuals $e_i = y_i - \hat y_i$, or
- internally Studentized residuals $r_i = e_i / [\hat\sigma\sqrt{1-h_i}]$?

# Residual definitions

For the fitted general linear model with hat matrix
$\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'$,
let $h_i = v_{ii}$ be the leverage of observation $i$ and
$\hat\sigma^2 = SE_\text{res}^2 = \sum e_i^2 / (n-k)$ the residual mean
square.

| Symbol | Name | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| $e_i$ | raw residual | $y_i - \hat y_i$ |
| $z_i$ | z-residual | $e_i / SE_\text{res}$ |
| $r_i$ | internally Studentized / "standardised" | $e_i / [SE_\text{res}\sqrt{1-h_i}] = z_i/\sqrt{1-h_i}$ |
| $t_i$ | externally Studentized | $e_i / [\hat\sigma_{(i)}\sqrt{1-h_i}]$ |
| recursive | Brown–Durbin–Evans | sequential (see §6) |

Cook & Weisberg (1982) notation correspondence:

| Cook & Weisberg | Vignette | R |
|---|---|---|
| $\hat\sigma$ | $SE_\text{res}$ | "Residual standard error" in `summary(lm)` |
| $v_{ii}$ | $h_i$ | `hatvalues()` |
| $r_i$ (internally Studentized) | $r_i$ (standardised) | `rstandard()` |
| $t_i$ (externally Studentized) | — | `rstudent()` |

# Theoretical setup

## Distribution of raw residuals

Under the model $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol\beta + \boldsymbol\varepsilon$
with $\boldsymbol\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2\mathbf{I})$:

$$\mathbf{e} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{V})\boldsymbol\varepsilon$$

So $\mathbf{e}$ is singular multivariate normal with

$$\operatorname{Var}(\mathbf{e}) = \sigma^2(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{V})$$

In scalar form:

- $\operatorname{Var}(e_i) = \sigma^2(1 - h_i)$ — **heteroscedastic**
- $\operatorname{Cov}(e_i, e_j) = -\sigma^2 h_{ij}$ for $i\neq j$ — **correlated**

Reference: Cook & Weisberg (1982) §2.1.2, p. 14, Eq. (2.1.3).

## Distribution of internally Studentized residuals

$r_i = e_i / [\hat\sigma\sqrt{1-h_i}]$ has

- $\operatorname{Var}(r_i) \approx 1$ — variance correction works
- non-zero off-diagonal covariance survives: rescaling diagonally
  does not zero off-diagonals of $\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{V}$
- additional weak dependence through the shared $\hat\sigma$

So studentization fixes the heteroscedasticity problem but **not** the
correlation problem.

## What this means for the SW null distribution

Shapiro & Wilk (1965) derive $W$'s null distribution assuming the
input is an **iid sample** from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$. Neither
$e_i$ nor $r_i$ satisfies this. Specifically:

- $-\sigma^2 h_{ij}$ off-diagonals are typically negative (sum-to-zero
  constraint of OLS residuals)
- SW applied to negatively-correlated draws **under-rejects** —
  i.e. it is conservative under $H_0$ (test rejects normality less
  often than the nominal $\alpha$)

Operational consequence: `shapiro.test(resid(model))` and
`shapiro.test(rstandard(model))` both deliver $p$-values that are
conservative approximations, not exact $p$-values. Switching between
the two does **not** restore the validity of the null distribution.

# Bibliography sweep (Zotero, 2026-05-29)

12 papers read in full (or to sufficient depth, flagged below). All
PDFs in `~/Library/.../storage/`. No web snippets cited (CLAUDE.md
rule). Cook & Weisberg (1982) Chapter 2 read in detail in earlier
session.

| # | Reference | BibKey | Pages read | Addresses residual choice? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cook & Weisberg, *Residuals and Influence in Regression* (1982) | `Cook:1982` | Ch. 2 | Yes — defines Studentized; gold-standard text |
| 2 | Kozak & Piepho, J Agro Crop Sci (2018) | `Kozak:2018` | Full | Yes — explicit recommendation |
| 3 | Shatz, Behav Res Methods (2024) | `Shatz:2024` | Full | Yes — implicit (uses standardised); supernormality caveat |
| 4 | Knief & Forstmeier, Behav Res Methods (2021) | not yet in bib | Full | Uses Studentized in workflow |
| 5 | Razali & Wah, J Stat Modeling (2011) | `Razali:2011` | Full | No — iid sample context |
| 6 | Royston, Appl Stat (1982) | `Royston:1982` | Full | No — iid methodology |
| 7 | Shapiro & Wilk, Biometrika (1965) | `Shapiro:1965` | 10 of 22 | No — iid "complete sample" |
| 8 | Anderson & Darling, Ann Math Stat (1952) | `Anderson:1952` | 6 of 20 | No — iid context |
| 9 | Lumley et al., Annu Rev Public Health (2002) | `Lumley:2002` | Full | No — CLT robustness focus |
| 10 | Fagerland, BMC Med Res Methodol (2012) | `Fagerland:2012` | Full | No — against gatekeeping |
| 11 | Ghasemi & Zahediasl, Int J Endocrinol Metab (2012) | `Ghasemi:2012` | Full | No — tests raw data |
| 12 | Franc, Prehosp Disaster Med (2025) | `Franc:2025` | Full | No — against gatekeeping |
| 13 | Das & Imon, Am J Theor Appl Stat (2016) | not yet in bib | Full | **Yes — direct counter-case: SW/JB on residuals "not appropriate"; recommends Rescaled Moments test** |
| 14 | Tanadini, *Linear Models 4: Residual analysis Lab*, ETHZ HS19 (teaching handout, no DOI) | **not citable** | Full (17 pp.) | Group B — "tests... should not be used"; graphical tools use standardised residuals |
| 15 | Kéry & Hatfield, Bull Ecol Soc Am (2003) | not yet in bib (DOI to verify) | Full (pp. 92–94) | No on residual scale; canonical "test residuals, not raw data"; permissive on formal tests |

## Bibliography defect found

The file
`storage/BP34C34R/Yap und Sim - 2011 - Comparisons of various types of normality tests.pdf`
is **mislabeled** in Zotero. Its actual content is
Ruiz & Carranza (2018), *Inteligencia emocional, género y clima
familiar en adolescentes peruanos* (Acta Colombiana de Psicología).
The genuine Yap & Sim (2011) paper is **not** in local storage. The
bib entry `Yap:2011` therefore points to a file whose content does
not match its metadata. Action: re-import the correct PDF before
relying on this citation.

## What each Group A paper says

- **Cook & Weisberg (1982)**, p. 18, Eq. (2.2.1): defines
  $r_i = e_i / [\hat\sigma\sqrt{1-v_{ii}}]$ as the canonical
  internally Studentized residual. "The Studentized residuals are
  used as replacements for the ordinary residuals in graphical
  procedures" (p. 20).
- **Kozak & Piepho (2018)**, p. 12 (Conclusion): *"Generally,
  standardized or studentized residuals are better for checking
  assumptions than raw residuals, because the latter may display
  heterogeneous variance even when errors have constant variance
  (though for the special case of a balanced one-way both are equally
  suitable)."* Reasoning is heteroscedasticity-based. Does not
  engage with the correlation problem.
- **Shatz (2024)**, p. 826, footnote 1: *"This assumption actually
  pertains to the models' errors, which cannot be directly observed,
  and which are therefore estimated using the residuals … this
  approach has some flaws, like the problem of supernormality, where
  residuals appear to be normal even if the errors are not normal
  … recursive residuals … may sometimes be better suited for
  diagnostic purposes than raw residuals."* Author uses standardised
  residuals in all diagnostic figures.
- **Knief & Forstmeier (2021)**, p. 2581 (Methods): "we further
  calculated studentized residuals ($R$), hat values ($H$) and Cook's
  distances ($D$)". Their main thesis is unrelated to the choice —
  they argue Gaussian models are robust to non-normality except in
  the presence of influential outliers.

## What Group B papers say

- **Shapiro & Wilk (1965)**, p. 592 §2.2: $W$ is derived for "a sample
  from a normal distribution with unknown mean $\mu$ and unknown
  variance $\sigma^2$" — iid.
- **Anderson & Darling (1952)**, p. 193 §1: tests
  "$n$ independent, identically distributed random variables".
- **Royston (1982)**, **Razali & Wah (2011)**: extend / compare W on
  iid simulated samples; not residuals.
- **Lumley et al. (2002)**, p. 166: *"Formal statistical tests for
  Normality are especially undesirable as they will have low power
  in the small samples where the distribution matters and high power
  only in large samples where the distribution is unimportant."*
- **Fagerland (2012)**, p. 6: *"We strongly advise against [using a
  test for normality to decide between t-test and WMW]."*
- **Franc (2025)**, p. 242: *"The reliance on normality tests as a
  preliminary step for determining whether to employ parametric or
  non-parametric methods is both theoretically unfounded and
  practically problematic."*
- **Ghasemi & Zahediasl (2012)**: applied SPSS guide; tests raw
  outcome variables in worked examples; recommends SW over KS for
  generic normality assessment.
- **Tanadini (ETHZ teaching lab, HS19).** States plainly that formal
  normality tests "should not be used to test assumptions about the
  errors of a linear model" (p. 14), for two reasons: an infinite
  regress of testing (each test has its own assumptions), and the
  power/sample-size asymmetry (low $n$ misses real non-normality, high
  $n$ flags trivial deviations). Its recommended graphical tools
  (`plot.lm()`, `plregr()`) use standardised residuals, but it takes no
  position on raw-vs-studentized for a formal test because it rejects
  the test outright. *Grey literature — internal reference only, not
  citable in the vignette or paper (no DOI, course handout).*

# Why studentization is not the fix

The user-flagged correction (2026-05-29):

> Observed residuals are neither independent nor homoscedastic. $r$
> corrects for the latter; the first is not fixed.

Restated formally:

| Choice | Variance | Independent under $H_0$? | iid normal? | SW null distribution applies? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Raw $e_i$ | heteroscedastic | no | no | no — conservative |
| Studentized $r_i$ | $\approx 1$ | no | no | no — conservative |
| Recursive $w_i$ | constant | yes (under $H_0$) | yes | yes |
| Errors $\varepsilon_i$ | constant | yes | yes | yes, but unobservable |

The Kozak & Piepho recommendation and the Cook & Weisberg / Shatz /
Knief & Forstmeier convention all favour Studentized for
**plot-alignment and variance-scale** reasons, not because the SW
null distribution applies. None of them claim Studentized residuals
satisfy SW's iid assumption.

# Recursive residuals — the one type for which SW is exactly valid

Brown, Durbin & Evans (1975), JRSS-B, 37(2), 149–192.

- Defined recursively: fit model to first $p$ observations, predict
  $(p+1)$-th, the standardised forecast error is the first recursive
  residual. Iterate to get $n - p$ residuals.
- Under $H_0$ (normal errors), recursive residuals are iid
  $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ — proper iid normal sample.
- Available in R via `strucchange::recresid()`.
- **Order-dependent**: requires a meaningful ordering of observations.
  In time series this is given; in cross-sectional regression like
  `visstat()`'s setting, ordering is arbitrary, so different orderings
  give different $W$ statistics. Not a clean answer for `visStatistics`.

Shatz (2024) footnote pointed at recursive residuals as the
theoretical fix; the order-dependence is why it isn't routine practice
in cross-sectional packages.

# Practical magnitude of the dependence

For balanced one-way ANOVA with $g$ groups of size $n_g$:

- Hat matrix is block-diagonal with within-block leverage $h_i = 1/n_g$
- Within-block off-diagonal: $h_{ij} = 1/n_g$ for $i\neq j$ same group
- Across-group: $h_{ij} = 0$
- So residuals are correlated only within group, with covariance
  $-\sigma^2/n_g$

For $n_g \geq 10$ the within-group correlation is $\leq -0.1$, weak
enough that SW's empirical type-I error stays close to nominal.

For simple linear regression, off-diagonal $h_{ij}$ depends on
$(x_i-\bar x)(x_j-\bar x) / \sum(x_k-\bar x)^2$. Structure is more
complex and not obviously small. This is where the correlation issue
is least benign — but the package does not use SW for routing in
Route 3 (only warnings — see `vignettes/visStatistics.Rmd`, Section
`sec:route-3`).

# Routing implications for `visStatistics`

Per `vignettes/visStatistics.Rmd` (verified 2026-05-29):

- **Route 1** (numeric response, categorical predictor): SW on pooled
  residuals is a routing gate, *but only when all $n_g \leq 50$*. For
  any group with $n_g > 50$, the normality gate is bypassed entirely
  on CLT grounds (Lumley, Fagerland).
- **Route 3** (two numeric variables, simple linear regression): SW
  result triggers a warning; it does **not** drive routing. Vignette,
  Section `sec:route-3`: *"If general linear model assumptions are
  violated, the corresponding p-values trigger warnings and
  recommendations, but no automatic model replacement."*

So the residual-choice question only affects routing in **one
sub-case**: Route 1 with $\min_g n_g \leq 50$. Everywhere else it's
a display/warning issue.

# Honest summary

1. SW assumes iid normal. Neither raw nor internally Studentized
   regression residuals are iid normal.
2. The correlation $-\sigma^2 h_{ij}$ is structural and survives
   studentization. Off-diagonals do not vanish under any standard
   transformation of $\mathbf{e}$.
3. Sign of correlation makes SW conservative on residuals; the test
   "behaves reasonably" not because the violation is small but
   because its direction is forgiving.
4. The literature that explicitly addresses the choice
   (Cook & Weisberg, Kozak & Piepho, Shatz, Knief & Forstmeier) all
   prefer Studentized residuals. The reason is plot-alignment and
   variance scaling, not test validity.
5. The literature that addresses the bigger question
   (Lumley, Fagerland, Franc) argues against SW as a gate at all.
   Package partially honours this via the $n_g > 50$ bypass.
6. Recursive residuals are the only iid-under-$H_0$ choice but are
   order-dependent and unsuitable for cross-sectional automation.

# Das & Imon (2016): the direct counter-case

Das, K. R. & Imon, A. H. M. R. (2016). *A Brief Review of Tests for
Normality.* Am J Theor Appl Stat 5(1):5-12.
doi:10.11648/j.ajtas.20160501.12. Read in full.

§4 ("Supernormality and Rescaled Moments Test", p. 10) names three
drawbacks of feeding residuals to a normality test:

1. **always correlated** — "even when the true errors are independent,
   their corresponding residuals are always correlated";
2. **non-constant variance** (heteroscedastic);
3. **supernormality** — the residual distribution "is always closer to
   normal form than is the probability distribution of the true
   errors, when the errors are not normal".

Studentization (`rstandard()`) fixes only (2). The correlation (1) and
supernormality (3) survive it. Crucially, Das & Imon attribute the
correlation to substituting residuals for unobservable errors — the
projection itself — not to model structure and not to interaction
terms. This is independent, textbook-grade confirmation that the
residual covariance is a first-order, structural problem present in
every general linear model fit, with or without interaction terms.

Their conclusion (p. 11): "both Shapiro-Wilk and Jarque-Bera tests are
not appropriate when we test normality of residuals in regression
and/or design of experiments. We recommend using the rescaled moments
test in this regard." The Rescaled Moments (RM) statistic of Imon
(2003) is a Jarque–Bera variant with a $c=n/(n-k)$ correction:
$$\mathrm{RM} = \frac{n\,c^3}{6}\Big[S^2 + \tfrac{c(K-3)^2}{4}\Big],
\quad c=\tfrac{n}{n-k}.$$

**Bearing on this package.** Das & Imon adds a third position distinct
from Groups A and B: SW/AD on residuals — raw *or* studentized — are
inappropriate, and the remedy is a regression-specific test, not a
change of residual scale. It therefore *weakens* the case for the
raw → `rstandard()` switch (Option B fixes one of three flaws) and
*strengthens* the Group B view (Option C) that the gate itself, not
the residual scale, is the substantive issue. Adopting RM would be an
architectural change (different machinery from SW/AD) — noted, not
recommended for this question.

# Kéry & Hatfield (2003): residuals, not raw data

Kéry, M. & Hatfield, J. S. (2003). Normality of Raw Data in General
Linear Models: the Most Widespread Myth in Statistics. *Bull Ecol Soc
Am* 84(2):92-94. Read in full. (Apparent DOI
10.1890/0012-9623(2003)84[92:NORDIG]2.0.CO;2 — verify before citing.)

Thesis: in general linear models (t test, regression, ANOVA, ANCOVA)
the normality assumption is on the residuals, not the raw response;
treating the raw response is "the most widespread myth in statistics".
Two worked examples show non-normal raw data with normal residuals.

Bearing on this package: confirms that `visstat()` correctly tests
residuals rather than the raw outcome, and supplies a citable,
correctly-termed "general linear model" precedent for the vignette's
framing. Silent on raw-vs-studentized residuals and on residual
covariance, so it does not touch those sub-questions. Permissive (not
hostile) on formal tests, so it is not a Group B gate-keeping source.

# Options for the package

## Option A: Keep raw residuals, document the limitation

- Minimal change.
- Add one sentence to vignette Section `sec:assumption-tests`
  acknowledging that the test is approximate either way.
- Honest about the heuristic nature of the gate.

## Option B: Switch to `rstandard()`, document honestly

- Align test input with the residual scale plotted (z-residuals).
- Cite Cook & Weisberg (1982) p. 18 and Kozak & Piepho (2018) p. 12.
- Must still say the residuals remain correlated, so the p-value is
  approximate.
- Code change at 5 sites; existing routing decisions unlikely to
  change at routing-precision level for worked examples (worth
  verifying with `devtools::test()`).

## Option C: Drop SW as a gate altogether

- Most aligned with Lumley, Fagerland, Franc, Shatz.
- Larger architectural change to `visstat()`'s decision logic.
- Out of scope for this question.

# Candidate vignette sentence (drafted but not applied)

For Section `\@ref(sec:assumption-tests)`:

> Both Shapiro–Wilk and Anderson–Darling assume an iid normal sample.
> Model residuals violate this regardless of the residual type
> chosen: raw residuals are heteroscedastic with variance
> $\sigma^2(1-h_i)$, and internally Studentized residuals correct
> this but remain negatively correlated through the
> $\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{V}$ structure of the hat matrix. The displayed
> $p$-values are therefore conservative approximations, used as
> routing screens rather than strict tests of normality
> [@Cook:1982, p. 18; @Lumley:2002].

# Open items

- **No edit applied** to `R/` or `vignettes/visStatistics.Rmd` from
  this discussion. Decision deferred to the user.
- Knief & Forstmeier (2021) is a useful Group A citation; add to
  `REFERENCES.bib` if any of the options above is adopted.
- Das & Imon (2016) is the strongest direct citation for the
  residual-in-SW question; add to `REFERENCES.bib` if Option A's
  limitation sentence or Option C is adopted.
- Re-import correct Yap & Sim (2011) PDF into Zotero before relying
  on `Yap:2011`.
- If Route 1 SW gate is kept, consider whether `rstandard()` should
  feed both SW and AD; both currently use raw residuals at the same
  code sites.

# References (read for this note)

- Anderson, T. W. & Darling, D. A. (1952). Asymptotic Theory of
  Certain Goodness of Fit Criteria Based on Stochastic Processes.
  *Ann Math Stat*, 23(2), 193–212.
- Brown, R. L., Durbin, J. & Evans, J. M. (1975). Techniques for
  Testing the Constancy of Regression Relationships over Time.
  *JRSS-B*, 37(2), 149–192. *Not read in full this session — cited
  here as the source of recursive residuals; theoretical claim
  verified via Shatz 2024 footnote which references the development
  via Kianifard & Swallow (1996).*
- Cook, R. D. & Weisberg, S. (1982). *Residuals and Influence in
  Regression*. New York: Chapman and Hall.
- Das, K. R. & Imon, A. H. M. R. (2016). A Brief Review of Tests for
  Normality. *Am J Theor Appl Stat*, 5(1), 5–12.
  doi:10.11648/j.ajtas.20160501.12.
- Fagerland, M. W. (2012). t-tests, non-parametric tests, and large
  studies — a paradox of statistical practice? *BMC Med Res
  Methodol*, 12, 78.
- Franc, J. M. (2025). The Misuse of Normality Tests as Gatekeepers
  for Research in Prehospital and Disaster Medicine. *Prehosp
  Disaster Med*, 40(5), 241–242.
- Ghasemi, A. & Zahediasl, S. (2012). Normality Tests for Statistical
  Analysis: A Guide for Non-Statisticians. *Int J Endocrinol Metab*,
  10(2), 486–489.
- Knief, U. & Forstmeier, W. (2021). Violating the normality
  assumption may be the lesser of two evils. *Behav Res Methods*,
  53(6), 2576–2590.
- Kozak, M. & Piepho, H.-P. (2018). What's normal anyway? Residual
  plots are more telling than significance tests when checking ANOVA
  assumptions. *J Agro Crop Sci*, 204(1), 86–98.
- Lumley, T., Diehr, P., Emerson, S. & Chen, L. (2002). The
  Importance of the Normality Assumption in Large Public Health Data
  Sets. *Annu Rev Public Health*, 23, 151–169.
- Razali, N. M. & Wah, Y. B. (2011). Power comparisons of
  Shapiro-Wilk, Kolmogorov-Smirnov, Lilliefors and Anderson-Darling
  tests. *J Stat Modeling Anal*, 2(1), 21–33.
- Royston, J. P. (1982). An Extension of Shapiro and Wilk's W Test
  for Normality to Large Samples. *Appl Stat*, 31(2), 115–124.
- Shapiro, S. S. & Wilk, M. B. (1965). An analysis of variance test
  for normality (complete samples). *Biometrika*, 52(3/4), 591–611.
- Shatz, I. (2024). Assumption-checking rather than (just) testing:
  The importance of visualization and effect size in statistical
  diagnostics. *Behav Res Methods*, 56, 826–845.
